title: Hand Rankings
subtitle: From high card to royal flush — knowing what beats what
intro: Every poker decision eventually reduces to comparing five-card hands. Texas Hold'em uses a fixed hierarchy: high card, one pair, two pair, three of a kind, straight, flush, full house, four of a kind, straight flush, and royal flush.

You always form the best five-card hand from exactly five cards — using zero, one, or both hole cards plus community cards. When two players tie on the rank of a hand, kickers (the side cards) or the board itself decide the winner.

Misreading hand strength is one of the costliest beginner mistakes. This lesson trains you to rank hands quickly, spot counterfeited pairs, and recognize when the board alone is the best hand for everyone.

Hand rankings are not just a memorization chart — they determine whether you are value betting, bluff-catching, or folding to aggression. In solver-backed play, nut hands (the best possible holding on a board) get bet and raised aggressively, while second-best made hands face reverse implied odds and thin value decisions. Kickers break ties on paired boards; playing the board splits pots. Recognizing when your full house loses to a higher boat, or when your flush is dominated, is the difference between a +EV call and a stack-dumping mistake.

primer.heading: The ranking ladder
body: Every showdown reduces to one question: whose best **five-card hand** is highest? Hold'em uses a fixed ten-rung ladder, from **high card** at the bottom up to the **royal flush** at the top.
You build your five from any mix of your two hole cards and the five board cards. Memorize the order cold — every value bet, bluff-catch, and fold depends on reading it instantly.

keyTerms.definition: A completed five-card hand (a pair or better), as opposed to a draw still hoping to improve.
The best possible hand on a given board — by definition it cannot be beaten.

primer.heading: Rarer hands win
body: The ladder is not arbitrary — it is sorted by **rarity**. The harder a hand is to make, the higher it ranks. A pair appears constantly; four of a kind almost never does, so quads win.

This is why a flush beats a straight: with 13 cards per suit, completing five of one suit is harder than finding five consecutive ranks. When you forget the order, ask which holding is **less likely**.

visual.title: The rarity rule
visual.text: If you are unsure which hand wins, the **less likely** holding is the stronger one. Rank is just the inverse of frequency.

primer.heading: Kickers break ties
body: When two players hold the **same rank** of hand, the **kicker** — the highest unused side card — decides it. Compare cards top-down until they differ.
Pair of aces with a king kicker beats pair of aces with a queen kicker. On paired boards especially, the kicker is often the whole ballgame.

keyTerms.definition: A side card that is not part of the made hand itself, used to break ties between equal-ranked hands.

example.prompt: You hold A♦ J♦, villain holds A♠ T♠, board A♥ 8♣ 5♦ 2♠ K♣. Who wins?
steps: Both make exactly a pair of aces using the A♥ on the board.
Neither completes a straight, flush, or second pair — so compare kickers.
Your best five is A-A-K-J-8; villain's is A-A-K-T-8.
The first three cards tie (A-A-K). Your jack beats his ten.
example.result: You win — the jack kicker outranks the ten.

primer.heading: Playing the board
body: You may use **zero, one, or two** hole cards. Sometimes the five community cards are already the best hand you can make — you 'play the board'.
When the board is the best five-card hand for everyone still in, the pot is **split**. A lone high hole card does not help if it cannot improve on the board.

keyTerms.definition: Using zero hole cards because the five community cards already form your best five-card hand.

visual.title: Watch for split pots
visual.text: If the board makes a straight or flush that no hole card can beat, every remaining player ties and **chops** the pot.

primer.heading: Counterfeiting and the nuts
body: Strong hands are **relative**. A full house is huge — unless a bigger full house is out there. When the board pairs, your trips or two pair can be **counterfeited**: overtaken by a higher version of the same hand.
Always ask what the **nuts** are on this board and how close you are to them. Second-best hands on scary boards win small pots and lose big ones — the essence of reverse implied odds.

keyTerms.definition: When a board card pairs and devalues your hand — e.g. a board pair hands everyone a higher full house.
The ace-high flush — the best possible flush whenever three or more of a suit appear on the board.

example.prompt: You hold 8♦ 8♠ on board 8♥ K♣ K♦ 5♠ 5♥. An opponent holds A♠ K♠. Who wins?
steps: Your best five: three 8s plus the board's kings — 8-8-8-K-K, eights full of kings.
Opponent uses the board's K-K plus their K♠ for three kings, then 5-5 — K-K-K-5-5, kings full of fives.
Compare the trips that anchor each full house: kings beat eights.
example.result: Opponent wins — kings full beats eights full, even though your hand is 'full' too.

primerNarration.title: What beats what
primerNarration.caption: Every showdown asks one thing: whose best five-card hand is highest? Hold'em sorts hands on a fixed ladder, from high card at the bottom up to the royal flush at the top. Learn the order until it is automatic.

items: High card, one pair, two pair
Full house, four of a kind

primerNarration.title: Rarer hands win
primerNarration.caption: The order is not arbitrary — it tracks how rare each hand is. Pairs show up all the time, four of a kind almost never, so four of a kind wins. When you forget which is stronger, just ask which hand is harder to make.

primerNarration.title: Side cards settle ties
primerNarration.caption: When two players make the same hand, the kicker decides it. A pair of aces with a king kicker beats a pair of aces with a queen. Compare the cards from the top down until they differ.

primerNarration.title: Sometimes the board wins
primerNarration.caption: You can use zero, one, or two of your hole cards. If the five community cards are already the best hand, everyone plays the board and the pot is split. A single high card does not help unless it actually improves on the board.

primerNarration.title: Strength is relative
primerNarration.caption: A full house is only as good as the bigger full houses it might be facing. When the board pairs, your hand can be counterfeited and overtaken. Always ask what the best possible hand is, and how close yours is to it.

visual.text: Eights full loses to kings full.

explanations.A: A straight beats three of a kind but loses to flush and full house.
explanations.B: A flush beats straight and three of a kind but loses to full house.
explanations.C: Correct — full house (trips + pair) beats flush, straight, and three of a kind.
explanations.D: Three of a kind is strong but below straight and flush.

remediation.question: Which beats a flush?

explanations.A: Two pair loses to flush.
explanations.B: Correct — full house beats flush.

remediation.question: Rank these from lowest to highest: flush, straight, full house.

explanations.A: Flush beats straight.
explanations.B: Correct — straight, then flush, then full house.
explanations.C: Full house is highest of the three.

questions.question: A royal flush is:
options: Five cards of the same suit any ranks
A♥ K♦ Q♣ J♠ T♥ mixed suits

explanations.A: Correct — A-K-Q-J-T all the same suit is the royal flush, the highest possible hand.
explanations.B: Any same-suit five cards is a flush; royal requires the top five ranks.
explanations.C: Mixed suits cannot form a flush.
explanations.D: Four aces is four of a kind, not a royal flush.

remediation.question: The best possible Hold'em hand is:

explanations.A: Four of a kind is beaten by straight flush.
explanations.B: Correct — royal flush (A-high straight flush) is unbeatable.
explanations.C: Full house loses to straight flush.
explanations.D: King-high flush is far from the nuts.

remediation.question: Which is a straight flush?

explanations.A: Correct — five consecutive clubs is a straight flush.
explanations.B: Missing the ten for a straight.
explanations.C: Nine of hearts breaks the flush.
explanations.D: Two pair, not a straight flush.

questions.question: Board: K♠ 7♥ 7♦ 3♣ 3♠. You hold K♦ Q♣. Your best hand is:
options: Full house, sevens full of threes

explanations.A: Correct — your two highest pairs are kings and sevens (K♦ K♠ and 7♥ 7♦) with a queen kicker: K-K-7-7-Q. Sevens outrank threes for the second pair.
explanations.B: Kings and threes ignores the higher pair: sevens (7♥ 7♦) beat threes, so the second pair is sevens, not threes.
explanations.C: Full house would need trips + pair; you have two pair only.
explanations.D: Two pair is better than one pair.

remediation.question: You have A♠ A♥, board 9♣ 9♦ 5♠ 2♥ K♣. Best hand:

explanations.A: Correct — A-A-9-9-K beats using only board pairs.
explanations.B: Nines and fives ignores your aces.
explanations.C: No trips on board for a boat.

remediation.question: Two pair means:
options: Two different pairs among your five cards
Two players each have a pair

explanations.A: Correct — e.g. K-K-7-7-x is two pair.
explanations.B: Hole cards need not both pair.
explanations.D: Board pairs alone can make two pair for you.

questions.question: You: A♦ J♦. Villain: A♠ T♠. Board: A♥ 8♣ 5♦ 2♠ K♣. Who wins?

explanations.A: Correct — both have pair of aces; your J kicker beats T.
explanations.B: Villain's ten is lower than your jack.
explanations.D: Only one pair of aces matters.

remediation.question: Same pair on board, you have K kicker, opponent Q kicker. Winner:

explanations.A: Correct — higher kicker wins when pairs tie.
explanations.B: Queen loses to king.

remediation.question: Kickers matter when:
options: Both players have the same hand rank

explanations.A: Correct — tied hand ranks compare side cards.
explanations.B: Flush rank is decided by highest card in flush.
explanations.C: Paired boards can still need kickers.

questions.question: Board: Q♠ J♠ T♠ 9♠ 8♠. All players checked to showdown. Result?
options: Player with A♠ in hole wins
Split pot — everyone plays the board

explanations.A: A spade in hole doesn't improve on a straight flush board.
explanations.B: Correct — the board is a straight flush; hole cards irrelevant.
explanations.C: Hole cards don't matter when board is best for all.

remediation.question: When the board is the best five for all players:

explanations.A: Hole cards don't beat the board if board is best five.
explanations.B: Correct — tied best hand splits.

remediation.question: You may use zero hole cards when:
options: The board alone beats any combo with your cards

explanations.A: Correct — 'playing the board' uses zero hole cards.
explanations.B: Folded players don't showdown.
explanations.C: Aces might still play if board isn't best alone.
explanations.D: Zero hole cards is legal when board is strongest.

explanations.A: Correct — three kings and two sevens is a full house.
explanations.B: Two pair only — need three of one rank.
explanations.C: Four sevens is four of a kind.

remediation.question: Full house is also called:

explanations.A: Correct — 'boat' is common slang for full house.
explanations.B: Wheel is A-2-3-4-5 straight.

explanations.A: Two pair needs two ranks paired, not three eights.
explanations.B: Correct — eights full of kings.
explanations.C: Trips is three of a kind without a pair.
explanations.D: Four of a kind needs four eights.

explanations.A: Correct — wheel: ace plays low as 1 in A-2-3-4-5.
explanations.B: Nine of hearts breaks the straight.
explanations.C: Gap at ten — not five consecutive ranks.
explanations.D: Even ranks skip — not a straight.

remediation.question: The 'wheel' straight uses ace as:

explanations.A: Ace-high is Broadway, different straight.
explanations.B: Correct — ace can be low in the wheel.
explanations.D: Ace is part of the straight, not kicker.

remediation.question: A straight beats:

explanations.A: Flush beats straight.
explanations.B: Correct — straight beats three of a kind.

questions.question: You hold 2♠ 3♠. Board: A♠ K♠ 7♥ 5♠ 9♦. Your hand is:

explanations.A: Pair of aces ignores the five spades available.
explanations.B: Correct — A-K-9-5-2 spades is an ace-high flush.
explanations.C: You completed the flush, not just a draw.

remediation.question: Flush tie is broken by:
options: Highest card in the flush, then next cards

explanations.A: Correct — compare top down through all five flush cards.
explanations.B: Suit of hole cards doesn't matter for tie.

remediation.question: Five hearts on board, you have no hearts. You:
options: Have high card only if board flush is best

explanations.A: You don't hold a heart but board gives everyone a flush.
explanations.B: Correct — with five hearts on the board and none in your hand, you play the board's flush; the winner is decided by whether anyone holds a higher heart, otherwise it ties.
explanations.C: Wrong — when the board itself is a five-card flush, you always use that flush; you are never reduced to just high card.

questions.question: You hold 8♦ 8♠. Board: 8♥ K♣ K♦ 5♠ 5♥. Your effective hand vs someone with A♠ K♠?
options: They win — kings full of fives beat eights full

explanations.A: You have eights full of kings (8-8-8-K-K), but the opponent makes kings full of fives (K-K-K-5-5), which is higher.
explanations.B: Correct — higher trips win the full house: the opponent's K-K-K-5-5 beats your 8-8-8-K-K.
explanations.D: Trip eights became second best after board paired.

remediation.question: Counterfeiting often happens when:
options: Board pairs and reduces your pocket pair's value

explanations.A: Correct — board pairs can 'counterfeit' your pocket pair.
explanations.B: Flush improves your hand.

remediation.question: Pocket tens on board T-K-K-5-5 makes:
options: Weaker full house than K-x with a king

explanations.A: Tens full loses to kings full.
explanations.B: Correct — opponent with a king has kings full of tens.

questions.question: Board: 9♠ 8♠ 7♦ 6♣ 5♥. Player A: A♠ 2♠. Player B: T♦ T♣. Winner?

explanations.A: A has 9-high straight (9-8-7-6-5); T makes ten-high straight.
explanations.B: Correct — T-9-8-7-6 beats 9-8-7-6-5.
explanations.D: No five spades for a flush.

remediation.question: Ten-high straight on 9-8-7-6-5 board needs:
options: A ten in hole or as part of best five

explanations.A: Correct — T completes T-9-8-7-6.
explanations.B: Suits irrelevant for straight.
explanations.D: Ace low is wheel, different board.

remediation.question: When both have straights, winner has:
options: Higher top card of the straight

explanations.A: Straights can differ in high card.
explanations.B: Correct — compare the highest card of the straight.

questions.question: You hold A♦ T♦. Board runs out A♠ T♠ T♣ 4♥ 4♦. Your best five-card hand is:
options: Full house, tens full of aces
Full house, fours full of tens

explanations.A: Correct — three tens with your pair of aces: T-T-T-A-A (tens full of aces). Pair the trips with the higher pair (aces), not the board's fours.
explanations.B: Two pair was true on the turn, but with three tens plus a pair you now hold a full house — tens full of aces.
explanations.C: You only have three tens, not four.
explanations.D: Fours full of tens would need three fours; you only have the two board fours.

remediation.question: Board T-T-4-4-x with hole cards A-T makes:

explanations.A: Correct — three tens and two fours on board = full house.
explanations.B: Board pairing upgrades beyond two pair.

remediation.question: When the board pairs on the river, your two pair can:
options: Become counterfeited — board may make a better hand for everyone

explanations.A: Correct — paired boards can counterfeit hole-card pairs.
explanations.B: Quads need four of a rank.
explanations.C: Rank can change when board pairs.

questions.question: Board: K♠ 7♠ 2♠ 9♥ 3♦. Player A holds A♠ Q♣. Player B holds Q♠ J♠. Who wins?
options: Player B — higher spade in hand
Player B — straight flush draw completed

explanations.A: Correct — A♠ makes the nut flush; Q♠ J♠ is a weaker flush.
explanations.B: Both have a flush; A-high beats Q-high.
explanations.D: No straight flush on this board.

remediation.question: Three spades on board. A♠ in hand vs Q♠ in hand — winner has:

explanations.A: Correct — highest spade in the five-card hand wins.
explanations.B: Q-high loses to A-high flush.

remediation.question: The nut flush on a three-suited board is:
options: The highest card of that suit among all possible hands

explanations.A: Correct — nuts = best possible hand on that board.
explanations.B: Any flush can lose to a higher flush.
explanations.D: Nuts is the best, not the worst.

explanations.A: Pair and two pair lose to straight.
explanations.B: Two pair loses to straight.

explanations.A: Correct.
explanations.B: That's any flush.

placementQuestions.question: Same pair, you A-kicker, foe Q-kicker:

explanations.A: Correct — ace kicker.
explanations.B: Queen is lower.

placementQuestions.question: Board is best five for all:

explanations.A: Correct — play the board.
explanations.B: Button doesn't auto-win.

explanations.A: Two pair is weaker.
explanations.B: Correct.

explanations.A: Broadway.
explanations.B: Correct — ace low.

explanations.A: Correct.
explanations.B: Boat beats flush.

placementQuestions.question: Higher straight wins when:
options: Top card of straight is higher

explanations.A: Correct.
explanations.B: Doesn't matter how many hole cards.
